title: The One Where No One is Ready

content.problemStatement:
Ross has to give an important speech at the museum and he wants all of his friends in the first row of the auditorium. Ross is punctual and gets ready on time.


However, Joey and Chandler are behaving like children and fighting over everything. On getting a firm shouting from Ross, both of them decide to get dressed but they are still angry at each other. Chandler decides to have the final laugh by stealing Joey’s underwear. This makes Joey mad and he decides to wear all the pants owned by Chandler.


This infuriates Ross even more. There is a time crunch and he wants Joey to wear pants of a single color.



Joey has NNN pants. Given a string SSS of length NNN, where SiS_iSi​ represents the colour of ithi^{th}ith pant. Joey is allowed to make up to KKK moves. To make a move, Joey has to do the following:


Pick a prefix (possibly empty or complete), say PPP from the string SSS.
Discard a suffix (possibly empty or complete) from PPP.
Add PPP back to the string.
For example, Let the pants be denoted by the string SSS = AABADAABADAABAD. In one move Joey can, pick a prefix PPP = AABAAABAAABA, discard the suffix ABAABAABA from PPP and finally, add PPP = AAA back to the string SSS. The final string becomes ADADAD.

After making up to KKK moves, Joey decides to wear the pants denoted by the final string, if all of them are of the same colour.

Find the **maximum number** of pants that Joey can wear such that all of them are of the **same colour**, after making at most KKK moves. If it is impossible to wear pants of the same colour even after KKK moves, print **-1**.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
For each testcase first line of input contains two integers N,KN, KN,K, the number of pants and the maximum number of moves.
Second line of each testcase contains a string SSS consisting of uppercase english letters. ithi^{th}ith character from the beginning denotes the colour of the ithi^{th}ith layer of pants from top.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line the maximum number of pants of the same colour that Joey can wear using atmost KKK moves. If it is impossible for Joey to wear pants of the same colour finally, output **-1**.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
S consists only of uppercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K — the length of the string and the maximum number of moves. The second line contains a string S of length N consisting of uppercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible number of pants Joey can wear after at most K moves such that all remaining pants have the same colour. If it is impossible to obtain a string consisting of a single colour, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: For the first move, Joey can pick the prefix with first 2 pants i.e. ABABAB, out of this prefix, Joey can discard the suffix BBB and add the remaining prefix i.e. AAA back to the string SSS. Now the string SSS is AACAAACAAACA. For the second move, Joey can pick the prefix with 3 pants i.e. AACAACAAC, discard the prefix CCC and add remaining prefix AAAAAA back to string.
Finally, the string looks like AAAAAAAAA, i.e. 333 pants of colour AAA.
Test case 2:  For the first and only move, Joey can pick the prefix ABABABABABAB, i.e. the entire string and discard the suffix BABBABBAB, and add the remaining prefix i.e. AAA back to the string.

solution.approach:
In one move Joey can delete any contiguous substring: pick a prefix up to the right end of the substring, discard the suffix that equals the substring, and keep the remaining prefix. Therefore up to K moves allow deletion of at most K disjoint intervals. After deletions the remaining characters must all be the same letter c. For a fixed colour c we have to cover all positions that are NOT c with ≤ K intervals while losing as few c‑characters as possible. Consider the binary view where 1 = position of a non‑c character (must be covered) and 0 = position of c (optional to delete). Let the indices of all 1’s be p1 … pm (m = number of non‑c characters). If m = 0 the whole string already consists of c and the answer is N. If K = 0 and m > 0 the colour c is impossible.

If K ≥ m we can delete each non‑c character individually, losing no c’s, so we can keep all occurrences of c.
Otherwise we must merge some consecutive 1’s into the same interval. Between two consecutive 1’s there are gap_i = p_{i+1} – p_i – 1 c‑characters. When we merge those two 1’s into one interval we are forced to delete those gap_i c‑characters. The total number of c‑characters that inevitably disappear when we use exactly one interval is the sum of all gaps between the first and the last 1. By allowing up to K intervals we may leave K‑1 gaps uncovered; we should leave the largest gaps uncovered to minimise loss. Hence:
    total_gap = Σ gap_i (for i = 1 … m‑1)
    loss = total_gap – sum of the (K‑1) largest gap_i values
    kept_c = count_c – loss
The best answer for the test case is the maximum kept_c over all 26 possible colours. If the maximum is ≤ 0 we output -1.
The algorithm runs in O(26·N) per test case (collecting positions and gaps) plus O(g log g) for sorting the gaps of each colour, where g ≤ N. This easily satisfies the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(26·N + 26·g log g) ≈ O(N) per test case, because the total size of all gap arrays over the 26 colours is at most N.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing positions/gaps of a single colour (reused for each colour).
